topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the brutal finality of a gavel strike. Subject line: Departure Announcement. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly. Dr. Albright. He was leaving. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I reread the message three times, the words blurring before my eyes. My stomach tightened into a knot, a cold, heavy pressure spreading through my chest. I slowly leaned back in my worn desk chair, the squeak of the springs sounding deafening in the sudden silence of my room. How was I supposed to navigate the next phase of my research without his guidance? A wave of dizzying uncertainty washed over me, making the familiar textbooks on my shelf seem alien and unreadable. I pushed a hand through my hair, my gaze fixed on the dusty windowpane, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, unsettling drift.

[story 2]

"Wait, what?" I managed to croak out, the sound thin and reedy. The words felt like they were catching in my throat. Professor Davies had just sent the notification. His departure. It was as if the ground beneath my feet had dissolved. My hands, resting on the keyboard, began to shake uncontrollably, a rapid, nervous tremor running up my arms. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. I paced the small confines of my office, the carpet muffling the frantic rhythm of my footsteps. Every familiar corner of the room seemed charged with an unfamiliar tension. My mind raced, cycling through scenarios—unanswered questions, sudden isolation, the looming void where expert direction used to reside. I stopped by the window, staring out at the campus quad, feeling a profound sense of disorientation, as if the world had suddenly tilted on its axis.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen, my breath hitching in my chest. The official announcement detailing Dr. Evans' resignation was stark and impersonal. A strange numbness began to creep over my skin, starting at my fingertips and spreading inward. It wasn't panic, exactly; it was more like a sudden, deep withdrawal. My focus fractured; the intricate structure of my thesis proposal seemed to dissolve into meaningless lines of text. I let out a slow, ragged exhale, the sound catching in my throat. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, but the sensation offered no comfort. My gaze drifted to the stack of annotated notes on my desk, and for a moment, I couldn't decipher the familiar shorthand anymore. A profound sense of displacement settled over me, leaving me utterly still, grappling with the sudden absence of a cornerstone in my academic world.
